subject: Economics
instructions: Five (5) marks will be given for each correct answer.
One (1) mark will be deducted for each incorrect answer.
If more than one option is chosen, then it will be considered as an incorrect answer.
Unanswered questions will be given no mark.

passages.passage: When income of a country increases, consumer spending increases. Spending on imported goods is also likely to increase. When imports increase, the demand curve for foreign exchange shifts to the right. There is a depreciation of the domestic currency. If there is an increase in income abroad as well, domestic exports will rise and the supply curve of foreign exchange shifts outward. On balance, the domestic currency may or may not depreciate. What happens will depend on whether exports are growing faster than imports. In general, other things remaining equal, a country whose aggregate demand grows faster than the rest of the world's normally finds its currency depreciating because its imports grow faster than its exports. Its demand curve for foreign currency shifts faster than its supply curve.

It is not very uncommon to come across instances where the government fixes a maximum allowable price for certain goods. The government-imposed upper limit on the price of a good or service is called a price ceiling. A price ceiling is generally imposed on necessary items like wheat, rice, kerosene, sugar etc. The objective of the price ceiling is to restrict the price of a good so that it becomes affordable for consumers to buy. However, it does not always generate the desired results. Most of the time, intervention by the government in the form of a price ceiling leads to various socio-problems.

questions.question:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-I:
(A) Increase in demand > Increase in Supply
(B) Increase in demand < Increase in Supply
(C) Increase in demand = Increase in Supply
(D) Decrease in demand < Decrease in supply

List-II:
(i) Increase in both equilibrium price and quantity.
(ii) Decrease in both equilibrium price and quantity.
(iii) Decrease in equilibrium price but increase in equilibrium quantity.
(iv) Increase in equilibrium quantity but no change in equilibrium price.
questions.explanation: When the increase in demand is greater than the increase in supply, it leads to excess demand in the market, which increases the equilibrium price and equilibrium quantity (A-i). When an increase in demand is less than an increase in supply, it creates excess supply in the market, which creates competition among the sellers and hence the equilibrium price falls, but the equilibrium quantity rises (B-iii). When the increase in demand is equal to the increase in supply, it neither creates excess demand nor excess supply, and hence, there is no change in the equilibrium price but equilibrium quantity rises (C-iv). Note: There is an error in the question; when decrease in demand is less than decrease in supply, there will be a rise in equilibrium price and a decrease in equilibrium quantity.
questions.source_error_note: There is an error in the question. When decrease in demand is less than decrease in supply, there will be a rise in equilibrium price and a decrease in equilibrium quantity. The matching for D-(ii) as given in the answer key is therefore questionable.

questions.question: Full employment in context of determination of income, implies ?

options.D: Everyone in the economy is employed.

questions.explanation: Full employment, in the context of income determination, implies that all resources are fully employed in the economy and that the output or income generated is at the maximum possible level. However, at the full employment level, there exists a natural rate of unemployment, which includes frictional, structural and seasonal unemployment. Full employment only implies the absence of involuntary unemployment, meaning that people who are able and willing to work do not fail to get a job at the prevailing wage rate.

questions.question: Lata likes to eat burgers and has already eaten two. Her marginal utility from the last burger she consumes is 90 utils. If the price of burger is Rs. 25 per unit and marginal utility of a rupee is 3 utils, then ?

options.A: Lata should increase the consumption of burgers to attain equilibrium.
options.B: Lata should have consumed lesser burgers to attain equilibrium.
options.C: Lata is in equilibrium so she should not change the level of consumption.
options.D: The given information is insufficient to determine the consumption behaviour of Lata.

questions.correct_option_text: Lata should increase the consumption of burgers to attain equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Consumer Equilibrium Condition: MUx / MUm = Px. Here MUx = 90 utils, MUm = 3 utils per rupee, Px = Rs. 25. MUx / MUm = 90 / 3 = 30. Since MUx/MUm = 30 > 25 = Px, the marginal utility per rupee from the burger is greater than its price. Lata is getting more utility per rupee than the price she pays, so she should consume more burgers to reach equilibrium.

questions.question:
In an imaginary economy, the planned spending falls short of planned output at a certain level of employment. Arrange the following consequences of the same in sequential order.
(A) Unintended accumulation of inventories.
(B) The employment level falls.
(C) The output and income fall and the equilibrium is restored.
(D) Producers plan to produce less in the next period.
questions.explanation: When planned spending (aggregate demand) falls short of planned output, it leads to an increase in inventory or an unintended piling up of stock (A). This discourages producers from producing further, and hence, they plan to produce less in the next period (D). A fall in production cuts down employment levels (B). A fall in employment will reduce output and income levels in the economy, thereby helping to restore equilibrium (C).

questions.question: Which of the following is a quantitative tool of credit control by the Reserve Bank of India?
questions.explanation: Open Market Operations (OMO) are a quantitative tool of credit control where the central bank buys or sells government securities in the open market to regulate the money supply. Selling securities reduces credit, while buying increases it. Margin requirements, Moral Suasion, and Direct Action are qualitative tools of credit control.

questions.question: Anand consumes lesser units of good X when its price falls but more units of X when his income rises. Which of the following statements is true about good X.

options.A: Good X is an inferior good for Anand but not a Giffen good.
options.B: Good X is a Giffen good for Anand but not inferior.
options.C: Good X is normal good for Anand.
options.D: Good X is both inferior and Giffen good for Anand.

questions.correct_option_text: Good X is both inferior and Giffen good for Anand.
questions.explanation: Good X shows the defining trait of a Giffen good—Anand consumes less of it when its price falls, violating the law of demand. This happens when the negative income effect outweighs the substitution effect, a feature unique to Giffen goods. Since all Giffen goods are a subset of inferior goods, good X must also be inferior. Although Anand consumes more of X when income rises, the price-based response (violating law of demand) is the decisive factor. Hence, good X is both inferior and Giffen.

questions.question: Suppose an initial cash deposit of INR 100 crores has been made in a commercial bank where a total credit of INR 500 crores has been created in the economy. The required reserve ratio in this country is..............
questions.explanation: Initial Deposit = Rs. 100 crores. Total Credit Created = Rs. 500 crores. Credit Multiplier = Credit Creation / Initial Deposit = 500/100 = 5. Also, Credit Multiplier = 1 / Required Reserve Ratio. Therefore, 5 = 1/RR, so Required Reserve Ratio = 1/5 = 0.20 = 20%.

questions.question: What is required to be subtracted from personal income in order to obtain personal disposable income?

options.A: Net current transfers by the government to the households.
options.B: Net remittances received by households from rest of the world.
options.C: Net tax payments by the households.
options.D: Nothing is to be subtracted as personal income is same as personal disposable income.

questions.correct_option_text: Net tax payments by the households.
questions.explanation: Personal disposable income refers to that part of personal income which is used for consumption and saving. To calculate personal disposable income, net taxes are subtracted from personal income. Personal disposable income = Personal income - Net taxes paid by households. Net current transfers from government and remittances are already included in personal income, not subtracted.

questions.question: If the average fixed cost of production is the fixed cost per unit of output produced. Then it will ............... .

options.A: Remains same at all levels of output.
options.B: Increases with increase in the level of output.
options.C: Decreases with increase in level of output.
options.D: First increases and then decreases as the output is increased.

questions.correct_option_text: Decreases with increase in level of output.
questions.explanation: Average Fixed Cost (AFC) = Fixed Cost / Output produced. Fixed cost remains the same at all the levels of output. Hence, fixed cost per unit of output falls as the output produced increases. For example, if TFC = Rs. 10: at output 1, AFC=10; at output 2, AFC=5; at output 3, AFC=3.33; at output 4, AFC=2.5. AFC continuously declines and never becomes zero.

questions.question: Deficient demand is said to exist in the economy when:

options.A: Aggregate demand exceeds aggregate supply at full employment.
options.B: Aggregate demand falls short of aggregate supply at any level of employment.
options.C: Aggregate demand exceeds aggregate supply at any level of employment.
options.D: Aggregate demand falls short of aggregate supply at full employment.

questions.correct_option_text: Aggregate demand falls short of aggregate supply at full employment.
questions.explanation: Deficient demand refers to a situation when aggregate demand is less than aggregate supply at the full employment level. It leads to a deflationary gap in the economy. It is not just at any level of employment, but specifically at the full employment level that deficient demand is defined.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is true?
(A) Gross domestic income is a subset of national income.
(B) Corporate profit tax is a factor in income earned by the government.
(C) The production of wheat by a farmer retained by himself for his self-consumption will be included in the national income of the country.
(D) All capital goods are producer goods, but all producer goods are not necessarily capital goods.
questions.explanation: GDI and national income are different concepts. Domestic income is based on the location of production, while national income is based on the residency of income earners (A is false). Corporate profit tax is not a factor income for the government, but it is a transfer income (B is false). Wheat produced by a farmer and retained by himself for self-consumption is included in the national income, as it is production carried out in the economy using productive resources (C is true). All capital goods like machinery, tools and equipment are durable producer goods. However, there are intermediate goods like raw material, fuel and power which are single-use producer goods and cannot be referred to as capital goods (D is true).

questions.question: Which of the following represents the borrowing requirements of the government for the purposes other than interest payments?
questions.explanation: Primary Deficit = Fiscal Deficit - Interest Payments. This means the Primary Deficit shows the government's borrowing excluding interest payments. It reflects how much the government needs to borrow for purposes other than paying interest, like capital expenditure or covering the revenue deficit.

questions.question:
Which of the following are included while calculating national income by expenditure method?
(A) Expenditure on single-use producer goods purchased by firms.
(B) Expenditure incurred by a private trust running an orphanage on the education of the children.
(C) Expenditure incurred by the government on building dams and bridges across the nation.
(D) Expenditure by foreigners on the final goods produced in the country.
questions.explanation: Expenditure on producing single-use producer goods is not included as these are intermediate goods; their value is already included in the value of final goods (A excluded). Expenditure by any trust or NGO for welfare is considered as private final consumption expenditure (B included). Expenditure by government on construction of dams is considered as part of Gross Domestic Capital Formation (C included). Expenditure by foreigners on goods and services produced domestically is classified as exports (D included).

questions.question: The value of the nominal GNP of an economy was Rs 5000 crores in a particular year. The value of GNP of that country during the same year, evaluated at the prices of same base year, was Rs 6000 crores. What is the value of the GNP deflator of the year.
questions.explanation: Nominal GNP = Rs. 5000 crores. Real GNP = Rs. 6000 crores. GNP deflator = (Nominal GNP / Real GNP) x 100 = (5000/6000) x 100 = 83.33%. The GNP deflator below 100 indicates that the current price level is lower than the base year price level.

questions.question:
Which of the following are correct about the short run cost concepts?
(A) Both the average cost and average variable cost curves are U-shaped.
(B) Average cost and average variable cost curves are parallel to each other.
(C) Marginal cost is equal to both average cost and average variable costs at their respective minimum levels.
(D) Average cost and average variable cost can never be equal to each other.
questions.explanation: Both AC and AVC are U-shaped curves due to the law of variable proportion (A is true). AC and AVC curves can never be parallel to each other, as the difference between AC and AVC is AFC, and AFC always falls as output increases, so the distance between AC and AVC falls (B is false). The MC curve intersects AC and AVC at their respective minimum points (C is true). AC and AVC can never be equal as AFC can never be zero (D is true).

questions.question:
Which of the following is/are a qualitative tool of credit control adopted by the central bank of a country?
(A) Repurchase agreement.
(B) Moral suasion.
(C) Open market operations.
(D) Margin requirements.
questions.explanation: Qualitative tools are the instruments used by Central Banks to influence the direction of credit in the economy. These include Margin Requirements, Moral Suasion, and Direct Actions. Open Market Operations and Repurchase Agreements are quantitative tools as they affect the volume of credit.

questions.question: There may be times when demand exceeds available output under conditions of high employment and thus may give rise to inflation. In such situations, restrictive conditions may be needed to reduce demand. This intervention by the government is considered as
questions.explanation: Economic stability means the absence of large-scale fluctuations in prices. Such fluctuations create uncertainties in the economy. The government can exercise control over these fluctuations through taxes and expenditure. For example, under inflationary situations, the government may discourage spending by increasing taxes or reducing its own expenditure. This is the stabilisation function of government.

questions.question:
A firm is operating in a market where it can sell more only by lowering the price of the good. Arrange the changes in total revenue of this firm in a sequential order.
(A) Total revenue falls.
(B) Total revenue starts from origin.
(C) Total revenue increases at a diminishing rate.
(D) Total revenue reaches its maximum.
questions.explanation: The market in which more can be sold by lowering the price is imperfect competition. In this market, the marginal revenue curve is downward sloping and total revenue curve is inversely U-shaped. Total revenue starts from origin (when output sold is zero, TR is also zero) (B). Total revenue increases at a diminishing rate as MR falls but remains positive (C). Total revenue reaches the maximum point as MR becomes zero (D). Total revenue starts falling, as MR becomes negative (A).

questions.question: The RBI can influence money supply by changing the rate at which it gives long term loans to the commercial banks. This rate is called?
questions.explanation: Bank rate is the rate at which the RBI gives long-term loans to commercial banks. It is a quantitative tool used to influence the volume of credit in the economy. When the bank rate is increased, borrowing becomes expensive, reducing money supply; when decreased, it encourages borrowing and increases money supply.

questions.question: M3 is the most commonly used measure of money supply. It is also known as aggregate monetary resources. Which of the following is not a component of M3?

options.B: Demand deposits by people with commercial banks.
options.C: Savings deposits with Post Office savings banks.
options.D: Net time deposits of commercial banks.

questions.correct_option_text: Savings deposits with Post Office savings banks.
questions.explanation: M1 = Currency held by public + Net demand deposits held by commercial banks. M2 = M1 + Savings deposits with Post Office savings banks. M3 = M1 + Net time deposits of commercial banks. M4 = M3 + Total deposits with Post Office savings organisations (excluding National Savings Certificates). Savings deposits with Post Office savings banks is a component of M2, not M3.

questions.question:
Arrange the following in chronological order, starting from the earliest.
(A) Publishing of 'An inquiry into the Nature and Cause of the Wealth of Nations'.
(B) The Great Depression.
(C) Publishing of The Economic consequences of the Peace.
(D) Publishing of 'General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money'.
questions.explanation: The correct chronological order is: (A) 'An Inquiry into the Nature and Cause of the Wealth of Nations' by Adam Smith - 1776; (C) 'The Economic Consequences of the Peace' by Keynes - 1919; (B) The Great Depression - 1930s; (D) 'General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money' by Keynes - 1936.

questions.question: The increase in income in an economy is four times more than the increase in investment. What is the value of marginal propensity to consume in this economy?
questions.explanation: Change in income is four times more than the increase in investments. Therefore, delta-Y = delta-I + 4 x delta-I = 5 x delta-I. Investment multiplier (K) = delta-Y / delta-I = 5. K = 1 / (1 - MPC), so 5 = 1 / (1 - MPC), meaning 1 - MPC = 1/5 = 0.2, therefore MPC = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8.

questions.question: If the entire additional income is saved in an economy, the value of investment multiplier will be
questions.explanation: If the entire additional income is saved, then MPS = 1 (and MPC = 0). Investment multiplier = 1/MPS = 1/1 = 1. This means that any increase in investment leads to an equal increase in income with no further multiplier effect, since nothing is spent/consumed.

questions.question: A consumer spends all his income of Rs 6000 on two goods - Food and Clothing and is buying 12 units each of both the goods. At the present level of consumption, the marginal rate of substitution of clothing for food is 3. The price of food per unit is Rs. 400 whereas that of clothing is Rs. 100. What should the consumer do to attain the equilibrium?

options.A: He should reduce the consumption of food and increase the consumption of clothing.
options.B: He should increase the consumption of food and reduce the consumption of clothing.
options.C: He should reduce the consumption of both the goods.
options.D: He shouldn't change his consumption level as he is already at equilibrium.

questions.correct_option_text: He should reduce the consumption of food and increase the consumption of clothing.
questions.explanation: In consumer theory, equilibrium is when MRS = Price Ratio. MRS of clothing for food = 3. Price Ratio = PF/PC = 400/100 = 4. Since MRS (3) < Price Ratio (4), the consumer values clothing relatively more than the market does. The consumer should buy less food and more clothing. By buying less food, its marginal utility increases; by buying more clothing, its marginal utility decreases. This causes MRS to increase until it equals the price ratio.

questions.question:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-I:
(A) Decrease in consumer's income
(B) Increase in price of Good X
(C) Equal reduction in price of both goods X and Y
(D) Increase in price of Good Y

List-II:
(i) Budget line becomes steeper.
(ii) Parallel and leftward shift in the budget line.
(iii) Budget line becomes flatter.
(iv) No change in the slope of the budget line.
questions.explanation: When the consumer's income decreases, the budget line shifts leftwards as they can purchase less of both the goods (A-ii). When the price of good X increases, the budget line rotates inwards along the X-axis, becoming steeper (B-i). When there is an equal reduction in the price of good X and Y, there will be an outward shift in budget line but no change in its slope (C-iv). When there is an increase in the price of good Y, the budget line rotates inwards along the Y-axis, making it flatter (D-iii).

questions.question:
In a particular country, there was a severe heatwave. Arrange the following in the sequence of order in context of the market of ice-creams.
(A) Rise in the price of ice cream.
(B) Supply will remain the same.
(C) Competition among the buyers of ice cream increases.
(D) The demand for ice cream increases.
questions.explanation: When there is a severe heat wave, the demand for ice cream increases, shifting the demand curve rightward (D). The supply remains constant (B). This will increase the competition among buyers due to excess demand (C). Excess demand leads to a rise in the price of the product (A).

questions.question: The ratio of increase in income to increase in investment is termed as
questions.explanation: The ratio of the increase in income to the increase in investment is called investment multiplier. It implies that as the investment increases, income increases multiple times. The number of times income increases due to an increase in investment is known as the investment multiplier (K). K = delta-Y / delta-I = 1 / (1 - MPC) = 1 / MPS.

questions.question:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-I:
(A) Non-monetary exchange
(B) Externalities
(C) Per capita income
(D) GDP deflator

List-II:
(i) Can be positive or negative
(ii) Price Index
(iii) Domestic services by housewives
(iv) Total income/Population
questions.explanation: Non-monetary exchange includes transactions that do not involve money exchange, like domestic services provided by housewives (A-iii). Externalities refer to benefits (positive) or harms (negative) experienced by a third party due to an activity undertaken by someone else (B-i). Per capita income is the ratio of total income to total population (C-iv). The GDP deflator measures the extent to which the price level has increased from the base year to the current year and is a price index (D-ii).

questions.question:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-I:
(A) Marginal rate of substitution
(B) MUx / MUy = Px / Py
(C) -(Px/Py)
(D) Monotonic Preferences.

List-II:
(i) Slope of Budget line
(ii) more of at least one good and no less of the other good
(iii) Law of equi marginal utility
(iv) Slope of indifference curve
questions.explanation: MRS is the slope of the indifference curve (A-iv). The condition MUx/MUy = Px/Py is the law of equi-marginal utility / consumer equilibrium condition (B-iii). -(Px/Py) is the slope of the budget line (C-i). Monotonic preference means a consumer prefers a bundle with more of at least one good and no less of the other (D-ii).

questions.question: According to the law of variable proportions, which among the following option is correct?

options.A: The marginal product of an input initially rises and then, it starts falling.
options.B: The marginal product of an input initially falls and then, it starts rising.
options.C: The marginal product of an input throughout falls.
options.D: The marginal product remains constant throughout.

questions.correct_option_text: The marginal product of an input initially rises and then, it starts falling.
questions.explanation: In the Law of Variable Proportion, the Marginal Product (MP) initially increases due to better utilisation of fixed resources (increasing returns phase), then diminishes as input increases further (diminishing returns phase), and eventually becomes negative when overuse leads to inefficiency (negative returns phase).

questions.question: Which of the following central problems deals with the choice of technique of production?

options.D: Problem of full employment of resources.

questions.explanation: The problem of choice of technique is known as 'How to produce'. This economic problem relates to choosing between labour-intensive or capital-intensive technique of production. In a free market, this decision is taken based on the least-cost method. The technique that is available at a lower cost is chosen.

questions.question:
During the period of COVID, there was an apprehension that the Indian economy might slip into recession. Therefore, to combat the situation, the Reserve Bank of India reduced the Repo rate by 110 basis points. Arrange the consequences of the same in sequential order.
(A) Increase in aggregate demand.
(B) Reduction in the market lending rate by commercial banks.
(C) Increase in money supply.
(D) Increase in borrowings by the public.
questions.explanation: When RBI reduces the repo rate, commercial banks also reduce the lending rate (B). Due to a fall in lending rates, borrowing becomes cheaper and increases the lending capacity of commercial banks, so public borrowings increase (D). This increases the money supply (C). More money supply leads to higher aggregate demand (A).

questions.question: A profit-maximizing firm will attain its equilibrium at that level of output where?

options.A: The excess of total revenue over total cost is maximum.
options.B: Where the excess of total cost over total revenue is maximum.
options.C: Where the excess of marginal revenue over marginal cost is maximum.
options.D: Where the excess of marginal cost over marginal revenue is maximum.

questions.correct_option_text: The excess of total revenue over total cost is maximum.
questions.explanation: The profit-maximising firm will produce the level of output where it attains equilibrium. For attaining the equilibrium level of output or maximum profits, the difference between TR and TC should be maximum. This is also equivalent to the condition where MR = MC (and MC is rising), but the fundamental definition is where TR - TC is maximized.

questions.question: The progressive taxation system seeks to achieve which of the following objectives of the government budget?
questions.explanation: Under a progressive tax system, the tax rate increases with the increase in income. The burden of tax is more on the rich and less on the poor. This tax system is meant to reduce the inequalities in the distribution of income and wealth, achieving the objective of redistribution of income.

questions.question:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-I:
(A) Purchase of stocks of a private company by the general government.
(B) Sale of equity of a public sector enterprise to private sector.
(C) Payment of interest by the government on loans from financial institutions.
(D) Stamp duty received by the government on property transactions in the country.

List-II:
(i) Capital receipts
(ii) Revenue receipts
(iii) Capital Expenditure
(iv) Revenue expenditure
questions.explanation: Purchase of stocks of a private company by the government increases assets, so it is Capital Expenditure (A-iii). Sale of equity of a public sector enterprise (disinvestment) reduces assets, so it is a Capital Receipt (B-i). Payment of interest by the government neither creates assets nor reduces liabilities; it is Revenue Expenditure (C-iv). Stamp duty received is a tax receipt that neither creates liability nor reduces assets, so it is a Revenue Receipt (D-ii).

questions.question: There are 2496 students in a school as on 1st April 2023. During the session, there were 186 new admissions whereas 43 students withdrew from the school. At the end of the session, there were 2539 students in the school, out of whom 1800 were boys and the rest were girls. In this case, which of the following figures represent a flow variable?
questions.explanation: Flow variables refer to the variables that are measured over a period of time. 186 students were added to the total count during the session. Since the number of students who took admission is measured over an entire session (a period of time), it is a flow variable. Stock variables (2496, 2539, 1800) are measured at a point in time.

questions.question: The saving function of a two sector economy is given as S = (-)100 + 0.2Y. The equilibrium level of income of this economy is 1000, then calculate the value of consumption at equilibrium?
questions.explanation: S = -100 + 0.2Y. Equilibrium level of income = Rs. 1000. Substituting: S = -100 + 0.2(1000) = -100 + 200 = Rs. 100. Using the identity: Income (Y) = Consumption (C) + Saving (S), we get 1000 = C + 100, therefore C = Rs. 900.

questions.question: Delhi has become the most polluted city in India. Due to this, how will the production possibility curve of Delhi be affected?

options.B: No effect on the production possibilities curve.
options.C: Downward movement along the same production possibilities curve.
options.D: Upward movement along the same production possibilities curve.

questions.explanation: Due to pollution in Delhi, human productivity falls. Rise in pollution levels leads to health problems such as breathing difficulties, skin issues, or lung cancer. This reduces the quality of human resources in the state. As the quality of resources deteriorates, the PPC shifts towards the left, indicating that less of both goods will be produced.

questions.question: The total revenue of a firm is increasing at a constant rate. Which of the following is true about this firm?

options.A: It is operating in an imperfect market and can sell any quantity of the good at given price.
options.B: It is operating in a perfect market and can sell any quantity of the good at given price.
options.C: It is operating in an imperfect market and can sell more only by lowering the price.
options.D: It is operating in a perfect market and can sell more only by lowering the price.

questions.correct_option_text: It is operating in a perfect market and can sell any quantity of the good at given price.
questions.explanation: If total revenue is increasing at a constant rate, it implies that marginal revenue (slope of TR) is constant. When MR is constant, the AR (price) is also constant. Prices remain constant in a perfectly competitive market. In this market, a firm can sell any quantity of output at the given or constant price.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is true about public goods?
(A) Public goods are non rival in nature.
(B) Public goods are non-excludable in nature.
(C) Public goods may be produced by the government or the private sector.
(D) Public goods and public production can be used interchangeably.
questions.explanation: Public goods are those goods which are non-excludable and non-rivalrous in nature (A and B true). These goods can be produced by both public and private sectors; however, the private sector is usually reluctant due to non-excludability and non-rivalry features (C true). Public goods and public production cannot be used interchangeably. Public production means production by the government, but not all government-produced goods are public goods, and not all public goods are produced by government (D false).

questions.question: If the income of the country and income abroad increase simultaneously, then the domestic currency will
questions.explanation: As the income of the nation increases, it increases the imports of that nation. This will shift the demand curve for the foreign currency to the right. Simultaneously, if the income of another country also increases, it will lead to an increase in the exports of the domestic country, shifting the supply curve of foreign currency towards the right. Hence, the domestic currency may or may not depreciate, as it depends on whether exports or imports rise at the greater rate.

questions.question: A country whose aggregate demand grows faster than the rest of the world's normally finds its currency
questions.explanation: A country experiencing a rise in aggregate demand faster than other nations implies that people will purchase more goods from abroad. This will increase the nation's imports. To import goods, there is a need to demand foreign currency. A rise in the demand for foreign currency will lead to a depreciation of the domestic currency.

questions.question: A decrease in income of a country leads to?

options.A: Leftward shift in the demand curve of foreign exchange.
options.B: Rightward shift in the demand curve of the foreign exchange.

questions.correct_option_text: Leftward shift in the demand curve of foreign exchange.
questions.explanation: If the income of the nation falls, this will reduce the import of goods and services, thereby lowering the demand for foreign currency and causing a leftward shift in the demand curve for foreign exchange. A leftward shift implies appreciation of domestic currency, not depreciation.

questions.question: Other things remaining same, if the income abroad increases, what is the likely effect of the same on national income of the domestic economy?

options.A: National income is likely to fall.
options.B: National income is likely to rise.
options.D: National income will initially fall and then rise.

questions.correct_option_text: National income is likely to rise.
questions.explanation: When income abroad increases, it is likely that exports of the domestic economy will increase. This will increase the aggregate demand of the domestic economy and hence the income or output is likely to increase.

questions.question: If the volume of imports of a country are growing faster than its volume of exports, its gross domestic product
questions.explanation: When the value of imports grows faster than the volume of exports, the net exports (exports minus imports) decrease or become negative. Net exports (X - M) is a component of aggregate demand and GDP. A fall in net exports causes the GDP to fall or slow down.

questions.question: Price ceilings are also known as...
questions.explanation: Price ceiling is known as maximum price fixation, as buyers and sellers are legally bound to buy and sell the commodity at the maximum price fixed by the government. It is fixed below the equilibrium price to keep goods affordable for consumers.

questions.question: Suppose the market determined rent for apartments is $200 per month. The government intervenes and sets the rent control at $250 per month. What is the likely impact of this intervention on market of apartments?

options.A: There will now be excess supply of apartments.
options.B: There will now be excess demand for apartments.
options.C: The market determined rent will now rise above 200 per month.
options.D: There will be no impact of this intervention on the market of apartments.

questions.correct_option_text: There will be no impact of this intervention on the market of apartments.
questions.explanation: Since the government's rent control ($250) is above the market-determined rent ($200), the price ceiling is non-binding. It has no effect on the market because landlords are already charging less than the controlled price. A price ceiling is effective only when it is set below the market price.

questions.question: Imposition of price ceilings usually results into?

options.A: Excess demand for the good in the market.
options.B: Excess supply of the good in the market.
options.C: Surplus availability of the good in the market.

questions.correct_option_text: Excess demand for the good in the market.
questions.explanation: The imposition of a price ceiling will lead to excess demand in the market because the price ceiling is imposed below the equilibrium price. At lower prices, quantity demanded is greater than quantity supplied, which creates excess demand or shortage.

questions.question: The market determined price of a good is Rs. 40. The government determines the price ceiling on the good as Rs. 25. Calculate the excess demand/excess supply it will cause if the demand and supply functions are Qd = 200 - p and Qs = 120 + p respectively.
questions.explanation: Qd = 200 - P and Qs = 120 + P. At price ceiling = Rs. 25: Qd = 200 - 25 = 175 units. Qs = 120 + 25 = 145 units. Excess demand = Qd - Qs = 175 - 145 = 30 units. Hence, there is an excess demand of 30 units at the price ceiling of Rs. 25.

questions.question: In order to be effective, the price ceiling should be determined

options.C: At the same level as of the market determined price.
options.D: Either above or below the market determined price.

questions.explanation: The price ceiling is effective only when it is imposed below the market-determined price. If it is imposed above the market-determined price, it will create a surplus of goods. Since there are no binding constraints on price reduction, the excess supply will cause the price to fall back to the market-determined price, making the price ceiling ineffective.
